title: Respiratory Conditions (other than tuberculosis and sleep apnea)
purpose: DBQs collect clinician-documented medical information VA may use when evaluating disability compensation claims.

diagnosis_current_condition: What condition are you claiming, and what diagnosis has a clinician given you?
When did symptoms begin, how have they progressed, and what treatment/medications do you use?
What records, tests, imaging, labs, or specialist visits support the diagnosis?
nexus_service_connection: What in-service event, injury, illness, exposure, or aggravation do you believe is connected?
When and where did it happen, what unit/job/exposure was involved, and what records or witnesses can support it?
If secondary, what service-connected condition caused or worsened this condition, and what medical evidence says so?
functional_impact_severity: How does the condition limit work, attendance, physical tasks, concentration, relationships, sleep, travel, or daily self-care?
What can you no longer do, do less often, or do only with breaks, medication, assistive devices, or help?
Give truthful examples using frequency, duration, severity, and flare-up details.

rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.frequency_duration: How often symptoms occur and how long they last, including daily baseline and episode/attack patterns.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.severity_scale: Best, typical, and worst severity using a clear scale when appropriate, such as pain 0-10.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.work_daily_life_impact: Concrete limits: sitting, standing, walking, lifting, sleep, missed work, concentration, hygiene, chores, driving, relationships.
rating_breakers_to_document_truthfully.flare_ups: How often flare-ups occur, triggers, duration, what gets worse, what you cannot do, and what helps.

recommended_user_inputs: Claimed condition and formal diagnosis, if known
Onset date/timeframe and service event, exposure, injury, or secondary condition theory
Treatment history, medications, devices, surgeries, hospitalizations, therapy, labs, imaging, or tests
Baseline symptom frequency, duration, and severity
Flare-up frequency, triggers, duration, severity, and functional loss
Work limitations, missed work, accommodations, daily activity limits, relationship/social effects
cheat_sheet_use.truthfulness_guardrail: Use this only to organize accurate personal facts. Do not exaggerate symptoms, invent diagnoses, or state medical causation unless supported by a qualified medical opinion.

script_templates.truthfulness_guardrail: Fill in only what is true. Skip wording that does not apply. Do not exaggerate, invent symptoms, stressors, exposures, test results, or medical nexus opinions.

default_options.frequency: Daily or several times per week
default_options.duration: minutes to hours, with worse days lasting most of the day
default_options.triggers: exertion, smoke, dust, cold air, allergens, infections, humidity, or fumes
default_options.limitation: walking, stairs, exercise, yard work, sleep, talking during exertion, and work pace
default_options.treatment: inhalers or respiratory medication if prescribed, nasal sprays if used, CPAP if prescribed, avoidance of triggers, and medical follow-up
default_options.flareCare: use prescribed medication, slow down, rest, avoid triggers, hydrate, and seek care if symptoms worsen
default_options.serviceEvent: [specific service event, injury, exposure, aggravation, or secondary condition]
default_options.currentDiagnosis: Respiratory Conditions (other than tuberculosis and sleep apnea)
default_options.workImpact: I have to slow down, avoid dusty or fume-heavy areas, take breaks after stairs or exertion, and may miss work during infections or flares.
default_options.dailyImpact: Stairs, walking, chores, sleep, and outdoor activities take longer and require pacing.
default_options.mainSymptoms: shortness of breath, coughing, wheezing, chest tightness, congestion, fatigue, or reduced exercise tolerance
default_options.evidence: service records, treatment records, VA/private records, medications, imaging/labs/testing, profiles, buddy statements, or lay statements if true

sections.title: Medical History / Onset & Nexus
sections.intent: Answer when/where symptoms started, what service event/exposure/aggravation may connect them, and what treatment has been tried.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: current respiratory/sleep/infectious diagnosis, onset, treatment, medication/device use, testing, and current symptoms; in-service event, injury, illness, hazardous exposure, aggravation, or secondary service connection timeline; inhalers/medications, CPAP if prescribed, imaging/PFT/sleep testing, specialist care, and trigger avoidance
sections.what_to_say_template: My claimed condition is {{currentDiagnosis}}. It first started or became noticeable {{onset}} after {{serviceEvent}}. Since then, the condition has continued with {{mainSymptoms}}. I have tried {{treatment}}, but symptoms still happen {{frequency}}. Evidence that may support the timeline includes {{evidence}}.

sections.title: Symptom Evaluation
sections.intent: Answer DBQ-style symptom questions with exact frequency, duration, severity, triggers, and condition-specific symptoms.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: shortness of breath, cough, wheezing, fatigue, sleep disruption, exertional limits, episodes, treatment use, and work/daily impact
sections.what_to_say_template: My symptoms are {{mainSymptoms}}. They happen {{frequency}}, usually last {{duration}}, and are triggered or worsened by {{triggers}}. On better days they are about {{bestPain}}; on worst days they reach {{worstPain}}. For example, breathing or sleep-related symptoms happen {{frequency}}, worsen with {{triggers}}, and can last {{duration}}.

subsections.title: Frequency & Duration
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Document frequency, duration, pattern, triggers, and whether symptoms are constant, intermittent, recurrent, or episodic.
subsections.what_to_say_template: My {{currentDiagnosis}} symptoms are {{frequency}}. They usually last {{duration}}. The symptoms I need documented are {{mainSymptoms}}. They get worse with {{triggers}}.
subsections.example_template: For example, breathing or sleep-related symptoms happen {{frequency}}, worsen with {{triggers}}, and can last {{duration}}.

subsections.title: Severity Scale
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Describe severity using measurable words, worst-day impairment, and any DBQ-specific severe-episode language that truthfully applies.
subsections.what_to_say_template: On better days, severity is about {{bestPain}}. On worst days, it reaches {{worstPain}}. Overall, I would describe it as {{severity}} because it interferes with {{limitation}}.
subsections.example_template: On a bad day, symptoms reach {{worstPain}} and I have to stop or reduce {{limitation}} instead of pushing through.

bullet_templates: Breathing: shortness of breath, wheezing, cough, chest tightness, congestion, fatigue if true
Triggers: exertion, stairs, dust/smoke/fumes, cold air, allergens, humidity, infections
Treatment: inhalers, nasal sprays, antibiotics/steroids, CPAP, oxygen, or avoidance if true

sections.title: Functional Impact / Occupational Assessment
sections.intent: Answer how the disability limits work, daily activities, relationships/social function, reliability, and worst-day functioning.
sections.dbq_wording_to_hit: shortness of breath, cough, wheezing, fatigue, sleep disruption, exertional limits, episodes, treatment use, and work/daily impact

subsections.title: Daily Activities
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Explain work limitations, daily activity limits, missed time, accommodations, safety concerns, and reduced reliability/productivity.
subsections.what_to_say_template: This affects work because {{workImpact}} It affects daily life because {{dailyImpact}} I can usually continue for about {{limitBeforeStopping}} before I need to stop, rest, modify the task, or get help.
subsections.example_template: A real-life example is: stairs, walking, dust/fumes, poor sleep, or exertion force me to slow down, take breaks, or avoid certain tasks.

subsections.title: Flare-Ups / Worst Days
subsections.dbq_wording_to_hit: Describe flare-up frequency, triggers, duration, worst-day functional loss, and what helps relieve symptoms.
subsections.what_to_say_template: Flare-ups or worst episodes happen {{flareUps}}. They are triggered by {{triggers}} and can last {{duration}}. During them, I cannot keep up with {{limitation}}, and I use {{flareCare}}.
subsections.example_template: During a flare-up, shortness of breath, cough, fatigue, sleep disruption, or congestion worsens and I {{flareCare}} before I can resume activity.

bullet_templates: Activities: stairs, walking distance, yard work, exercise, talking while walking, sleep
Work locations: dust, fumes, smoke, heat/cold, masks/respirators, physical tasks
Pacing: stop after {{limitBeforeStopping}} or after stairs/walking if true
Worst days: rescue medication, rest, missed work, urgent care, or staying inside if true
